Fnrn PolrrrcAr- PnacrrcEs Covrurs sroN
428 J Street. Suite 620. Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
November 5,2002
Lavra C. Kuhn, Community Development Director
City of Scotts Valley
Planning Department
One Civic Center Drive
Scotts Valley, CA 95066
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-02-192
Dear Ms. Kuhn:
Re:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act').1
Please note that we can only advise prospectively and we cannot comment on any
past conduct.2 You stated that you have noiparticipated in any matters that are the
subject of this request since you made a down payment and opened escrow on the home
you purchased on August 2,2002.
QUESTTONS
1. May you participate in decisions regarding the relocation of two propane storage and
distribution facilities since you own residential property approximately 2,000 feet
from the Geyer Quarry relocation site?
2. May you participate in decisions regarding the Geyer Quarry acquisition as a
mitigation bank for the Habitat Conservation Plan since you own residential property
approximately 2,000 feet from the Geyer Quarry?
I Government Code sections 8 1000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Please note that the Commission does not provide advice relating to past conduct. (Regulation
18329(bX8XA).) Therefore, any conclusions contained herein apply only to prospective actions.
r€,


File No. A-02-192
Page No. 2
CONCLUSIONS
1. Since it is presumpdthat the decisions to relocate the propane gas tanks and
distribution facility to the Geyer Quarry will not have a material financial effect on
your residential property, you may participate in the decisions. Whether this
presumption is rebutted is a factual determination that a public offrcial must make.
You should examine the specific factors in regulation 18705.2(b) that would rebut the
presumption of materiality based on your facts. If you find that there will be a
material financial effect on your property, you may not participate in the relocation
decisions.
2. Since the "public generally" exception does not apply to the decision to acquire the
Geyer Quarry as a mitigation bank for a habitat conservation plan, you may not
participate in decisions pertaining to the Habitat Conservation Plan.
FACTS
You are the community development director/deputy city manager for the City of
Scotts Valley. Scotts Valley's current population is 11,600 persons. You recently
purchased a home, which is located just outside of the corporate limits of Scotts Valley,
but within the jurisdiction of the County of Santa Cruz. Your escrow closed on August 2,
2002.
The City of Scotts Valley has been working with the County of Santa Cruzto
acquire a privately owned property known as the Geyer Quarry. This site is located
approximately 2,000 feet from your home. The acquisition of the site by the city and
county will address several issues, two of which may create a potential conflict of interest
for you. You have stated that although both decisions concem the Geyer Quarry site,
they are two independent decisions that are neither alternatives nor interrelated. This site
could serve both purposes or either purpose independently. You have requested our
advice on the two issues identified below.
Propane Gas Company Relocation
The City of Scotts Valley is working to relocate two propane storage and
distribution facilities to a new location. The Geyer Quarry site is a prime candidate for
the relocation. The city has been attempting to relocate the propane facilities due to the
congested area suffounding their current locations and the significant safety threat that
they pose. A report from the Scotts Valley Fire District Board was submitted to the city
recommending relocation and identiffing the risks of the propane facilities. Your
concern is that the proposed propane company relocation could potentially impact
property values in the immediate residential area due to the introduction of bulk storage
(over 60,000 gallons) and distribution of a highly flammable gas.


File No. A-02-192
Page No. 3
Your role in the process is to assist the propane companies with the relocation and
to negotiate with the county regarding the acquisition and future permitting of the site.
You are also coordinating with the county on purchase of surrounding parcels. You
stated that you have not participated in these matters and are awaiting our advice.
You expressed concerns about the impact on properfy values in your residential
area due to the risks of storing and distributing highly flammable gas nearby. In fact, the
Scotts Valley Fire District Chief recommended to the city council that development
between a 500 and 1,500 foot radius of the existing tanks be reskicted to non-residential
use. Although this recommendation has not been formally accepted by the city council,
the city is meeting with the fire chief to discuss options for implementation which will
then be forwarded to the city council. You do not have information that the fire chief has
made this recommendation to the relocation site.
Habitat Conservation Plan:
The Geyer Quarry site is also being targeted for acquisition to provide a
mitigation bank site for a Habitat Conservation Plan ("HCP") for local endangered
species. The southem portion of Scotts Valley and the surrounding areas of the
unincorporated areas of Santa Cruz County are host to several endangered plants and
insects. Currently, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service has determined that any
construction involving ground disturbance could result in "take," particularly for the
Mt. Hermon June Beetle, an endangered insect. Any construction which results in "take"
is a violation of the Endangered Species Act. In order to allow construction to occur, an
HCP is required, which involves the setting aside of open space lands to serve as a
mitigation bank for the allowed construction.
Your role in the process to date has been to coordinate with the county on the
development of the HCP and to assist in the acquisition of the Geyer Quarry. Without
acquisition of a mitigation banking site, construction activities will continue to be
delayed, and may force individual property owners to attempt to obtain their own
individual HCP. Such plans can take years to process and are very costly.
Your home is located in an area that may contain the Mt. Hermon June Beetle,
and you are considering adding on to your home. The Geyer Quarry acquisition as a
mitigation bank for the HCP may have an impact on the property value of your home as
well as the cost and timing for obtaining permits to do future expansion work on your
home.
You also stated that ten percent or more of the population of the city and ten
percent or more of all property owners or homeowners in the city are affected by the June
Beetle. You believe that decisions regarding the HCP would affect your economic
interests in substantially the same maruler as this significant segment of the population
and property owners.


File No. A-02-192
Page No. 4
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest. In order to determine whether the
prohibition in section 87100 applies to a given decision, regulation 18700 provides an
eight-step analysis.
Steps (1) and (2) Is the individual a public official and will he/she be making,
participating in making, using or attempting to use his/her official position to
influence a governmental decision?
As the community development director/deputy city manager for the City of
Scotts Valley, you are a public official. Youhave been asked to participate in making two
different governmental decisions regarding the Geyer Quarry site.
Step (3) Identify the official's economic interests.
Under section 87103 of the Act, there are six different types of economic interests
that may result in a conflict of interest for a public official. The interests identified in
your advice request include residential property and personal finances:
Real Property: Apublic official has an economic interest in any real property in
which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more in fair
market value. (Section 87103(b); reg. 18703.2(a).) When the property was in escrow,
which closed on August 2,2002, you had an interest in real property due to the
$5,000 deposit. The option to acquire an interest in real property is an economic
interest under the Act. (Section 82033.) Since escrow has closed, you now own the
home.
Personal Finances: Under section 87103 and regulation 18703.5, a public official has
an economic interest in his or her personal finances and those of his or her immediate
family. A governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the
decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the
official or his or her immediate family increasing or decreasing.
Step (4) For each of the public official's economic interests, determine whether that
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision which the
public official will be making, participating in making, using or attempting to use
his/her official position to influence.
Real property is directly involved in a decision if it is the subject of a
goveflrmental decision or if any part of the public official's real property is located within
500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the real property which is the
subject of the govemmental decision. @egulation 18704.2(a).) Since your residential
property is approximately 2,000 feet from the Geyer Quarry, your real property interest is
indirectly involved in the decision. @egulation 18705.2(b).)


File No. A-02-192
Page No. 5
"A public official or his or her immediate family are deemed to be directly
involved in a governmental decision which has any financial effect on his or her personal
finances or those of his or her immediate family." (Regulation 18704.5.)
Steps (5) and (6) WiU the financial effect of the decision on the official's economic
interest be material and reasonably foreseeable?
After determining a public official's economic interest, it must be decided
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect
on any of those economic interests. First, the applicable standard of materiality must be
found. Then it must be determined if it is reasonably foreseeable that the effect of the
decision will reach the materiality threshold. Regulation 18706 states: "[a] material
financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable ... if it is substantially
likely that one or more of the materiality standards [citation] applicable to that economic
interest will be met as a result of the govemmental decision."
The materiality standard for real property indirectly involved in a governmental
decision is set forth in regulation 18705.2(b), as follows:
"(b) Indirectly involved real property interests.
(1) Real property, other than leaseholds. The financial effect of a
govemmental decision on real property which is indirectly
involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be
material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are
specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its
financial effect, and the nature of the real property in which the
public official has an economic interest, which make it reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have amateial financial effect
on the real property in which the public official has an interest.
Examples of specific circumstances that will be considered
include, but are not limited to, circumstances where the decision
affects:
(A) The development potential or income producing potential
of the real property in which the official has an economic interest;
(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;
(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not
limited to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of
use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the
neighborhood."
Propane Gas Comoanv Relocation
The Geyer Quarry site is a prime candidate for relocation of two propane storage
tanks and distribution facilities. Because your property is approximately 2,000 feet from
the relocation site, the financial effect of the decision on your property is presumed not to


File No. A-02-192
Page No. 6
be material. However, this presumption can be rebutted by specific circumstances that
make it reasonably foreseeable that there will be a material financial effect on your
property. (Regulation 1 8705.2(b)(1 ).)
Regulation I 8705.2(b)( I XA)-(C) address circumstances to consider when
evaluating the financial effect of a decision on property interests. These include the
development potential of the property and the character of the neighborhood, i.e. traffrc,
intensity of use, noise level and air emissions. You specifically expressed concems about
the impact on property values in your residential area due to the risks of storing over
60,000 gallons of a highly flammable gas nearby. Due to the proximity of the propane
tanks relocation site to your residence, all the factors in regulation 18705.2(bXl)(A)-(C)
need to be examined to determine if the presumption of materiality is rebutted. Whether
this presumption is rebutted is a factual determination which, ultimately, a public official
must decide.
We have previously advised that evidence considered in evaluating the
presumption regarding materiality under regulation 18705.2 must consist of reasonable
and objective facts, and that any conclusion reached regarding the presumption must be
objectively defensible. (O'Harra Advice Letter, No. A-00-174.)
We cannot make the necessary factual determination regarding the presumption.
You must apply your specific facts to the above factors in the regulation. If you conclude
that there is no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your property interest,
you may participate in the decision. Conversely, if you conclude that there will be a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your property interest, you may not
participate in the decision. In addition, you may not participate in any decisions
regarding a different relocation site until such time as the Geyer Quarry is no longer
being considered because these decisions would be considered interrelated.3
Habitat Conservation Plan
The Geyer Quarry site is also being targeted for acquisition to provide a
mitigation bank site as a habitat conservation plan for local endangered species in the
area,particularly the Mt. Hermon June Beetle, an endangered insect. Since your home is
located in an area that may contain the beetle, your ability to do an expansion on your
3 For purposes of determining conflicts of interest under the Act, govemmental decisions are
analyzed independently to determine if there will be a foreseeable and material financial effect on a public
official's economic interest. (In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.) Therefore, under certain circumstances,
a public official disqualified from one decision may participate in other related decisions, provided that the
official's participation in the latter does not affect the decision in which he or she has a disqualifying
conflict of interest. ($ueeney Advice Letter, No. A-89-639.) If a governmental decision may be logically
segregated from other related decisions, the public body may procedurally segregate the decision prior to
allowing the public official with a related disqualifying conflict of interest to participate in the decision-
making ptocess. However, where decisions are too interrelated to be considered separately, the official's
conflict in one decision will be disqualifying for the other. (Kilian Advice Letter, No. 4-89-522; Miller
Advice Letter, No. A-82-119.)


File No. A-02-192
Page No. 7
home may be affected. You stated that the Geyer Quarry acquisition as a mitigation bank
for the HCP could have an impact on the property value of your home as well as the cost
and timing for obtaining permits to do future expansion work. You also stated that
without acquisition of a mitigation banking site, construction activities will be delayed
and may force individual property owners to attempt to obtain their own individual HCP.
Such plans can take years to process and are very costly. You stated that personal costs
could exceed $250 in ayear.
Regulation 18705.5(a) imposes a materiality standard of $250 in a twelve-month
period on the official's personal finances. It states as follows:
"a) A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal
finances is material if it is at least $250 in arry l2-month period. When
determining whether a govemmental decision has amateial financial effect on a
public official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a
financial effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the
official, nor a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets
and liabilities of a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect
investment interest shall be considered."
Since you stated that the decision regarding the acquisition of the quarry as a
mitigation bank for the HCP could have a very costly effect, certainly in excess of $250,
on your ability to obtain permits for the expansion work on your home, it is reasonably
foreseeable that there will be amateial financial effect on yourpersonal finances.
Therefore, we conclude that the decision to acquire the Geyer Quarry for a
mitigation bank will have a reasonably foreseeable mateial financial effect on your
personal finances, and you may not participate in any of these decisions.
Step (7) "Public Generally" Exception
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate under the "public generally" exception. For this exception to apply, the
decision must affect each of the official's economic interests in substantially the same
manner as it would affect a significant segment of the public. (Regulation 18707.)
Regulation 18707.l sets forth the general rule for the'public generally"
exception, as follows:
"(a) Except as provided in Government Code sections 87102.6 and
87103.5, the material financial effect of a governmental decision on a
public official's economic interests is indistinguishable from its effect on
the public generally ifboth subdivisions (b)(1) and (b)(2) of this
regulation apply.
(b) Significant Segments and Indistinguishable Effects.


File No. A-02-192
Page No. 8
(1) Significant Segment. The govemmental decision will affect a
'significant segment' of the public generally if any of the following are
affected as set forth below:
(A) Individuals. For decisions that affect the personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities of a public official or a member of his or her
immediate family, or that affect an individual who is a source of income or
a source of gifts to a public official, the decision also affects:
(i) Ten percent or more of,the population in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency or the district the official represents; or
(ii) 5,000 individuals who are residents of the jurisdiction.
(B) Real Property. For decisions that affect a public official,s real
property interest, the decision also affects:
(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the official represents; or
(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jwisdiction of the
official's a5ency.
fl...fl
(2) substantially the same Manner: The govemmental decision will
affect a public official's economic interest in substantially the same
manner as it will affectthe significant segment identified in subdivision
(bxl) of this regulation."
Propane Gas Companv Relocation
Youprovided facts regarding the "public generally" exception that
apply to the HCP decision. You stated that the same facts do not apply to the
propane tanks relocation. Therefore, the "public generally" exception does not
awly to the decision to relocate the propane tanks to the Geyer euarry site.
Habitat Conservation Plan
You stated that ten percent or more of both the population and the homeowners or
property owners in the jurisdiction are affected by the June Beetle habitat and the HCp
requirements. Therefore, you concluded that asignificant segment is affected and the
HCP decisions will affect your economic interest in substantially the same manner as the
identified significant segment of the public. Howeveq you have not provided facts
supporting your conclusion. Your govemmental decisions pertain to acquiring the Geyer
Quarry as a mitigation bank for the HCP. Without acquisition, construction activities
will be delayed and certain individual property owners may have to obtain individual
HCPs, which may be very costly. You have not indicated how many property owners
may have to seek individual HCPs, but it does not appear that this number includes l0%
or more of the population, homeowners or property owners. Since subdivision (b)(2) is
not satisfied, the 'public generally" exception cannot be applied to the decision regarding
the Geyer Quarry acquisition as a mitigation bank for the HCp.


File No. A-02-192
Page No. 9
Step (8) Legally Required Participation
Step eight is an exception that applies when the official is legally required to
participate in the decision. You have not provided any facts to suggest that this exception
applies.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By
,Legal Division
JS jg

1. May you participate in decisions regarding the relocation of two propane storage and
distribution facilities since you own residential property approximately 2,000 feet
from the Geyer Quarry relocation site?

mitigation bank for the Habitat Conservation Plan since you own residential property
approximately 2,000 feet from the Geyer Quarry?

18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

18329(bX8XA).) Therefore, any conclusions contained herein apply only to prospective actions.

1. Since it is presumpdthat the decisions to relocate the propane gas tanks and
distribution facility to the Geyer Quarry will not have a material financial effect on
your residential property, you may participate in the decisions. Whether this

decisions.
2. Since the "public generally" exception does not apply to the decision to acquire the
Geyer Quarry as a mitigation bank for a habitat conservation plan, you may not
participate in decisions pertaining to the Habitat Conservation Plan.

You are the community development director/deputy city manager for the City of
Scotts Valley. Scotts Valley's current population is 11,600 persons. You recently
purchased a home, which is located just outside of the corporate limits of Scotts Valley,
but within the jurisdiction of the County of Santa Cruz. Your escrow closed on August 2,

for you. You have stated that although both decisions concem the Geyer Quarry site,
they are two independent decisions that are neither alternatives nor interrelated. This site
could serve both purposes or either purpose independently. You have requested our
advice on the two issues identified below.

The City of Scotts Valley is working to relocate two propane storage and
distribution facilities to a new location. The Geyer Quarry site is a prime candidate for

(over 60,000 gallons) and distribution of a highly flammable gas.
Your role in the process is to assist the propane companies with the relocation and
to negotiate with the county regarding the acquisition and future permitting of the site.
You are also coordinating with the county on purchase of surrounding parcels. You
stated that you have not participated in these matters and are awaiting our advice.
You expressed concerns about the impact on properfy values in your residential
area due to the risks of storing and distributing highly flammable gas nearby. In fact, the
Scotts Valley Fire District Chief recommended to the city council that development
between a 500 and 1,500 foot radius of the existing tanks be reskicted to non-residential
use. Although this recommendation has not been formally accepted by the city council,
the city is meeting with the fire chief to discuss options for implementation which will
then be forwarded to the city council. You do not have information that the fire chief has
made this recommendation to the relocation site.
Habitat Conservation Plan:
The Geyer Quarry site is also being targeted for acquisition to provide a

Your role in the process to date has been to coordinate with the county on the

delayed, and may force individual property owners to attempt to obtain their own
individual HCP. Such plans can take years to process and are very costly.
Your home is located in an area that may contain the Mt. Hermon June Beetle,
and you are considering adding on to your home. The Geyer Quarry acquisition as a
mitigation bank for the HCP may have an impact on the property value of your home as
well as the cost and timing for obtaining permits to do future expansion work on your
home.
You also stated that ten percent or more of the population of the city and ten
percent or more of all property owners or homeowners in the city are affected by the June
Beetle. You believe that decisions regarding the HCP would affect your economic
interests in substantially the same maruler as this significant segment of the population
and property owners.
sections.analysis:
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest. In order to determine whether the
prohibition in section 87100 applies to a given decision, regulation 18700 provides an
eight-step analysis.
Steps (1) and (2) Is the individual a public official and will he/she be making,
participating in making, using or attempting to use his/her official position to
influence a governmental decision?
As the community development director/deputy city manager for the City of
Scotts Valley, you are a public official. Youhave been asked to participate in making two
different governmental decisions regarding the Geyer Quarry site.
Step (3) Identify the official's economic interests.
Under section 87103 of the Act, there are six different types of economic interests
that may result in a conflict of interest for a public official. The interests identified in

Real Property: Apublic official has an economic interest in any real property in
which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more in fair
market value. (Section 87103(b); reg. 18703.2(a).) When the property was in escrow,
which closed on August 2,2002, you had an interest in real property due to the
$5,000 deposit. The option to acquire an interest in real property is an economic
interest under the Act. (Section 82033.) Since escrow has closed, you now own the
home.
Personal Finances: Under section 87103 and regulation 18703.5, a public official has
an economic interest in his or her personal finances and those of his or her immediate
family. A governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the
decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the

Step (4) For each of the public official's economic interests, determine whether that
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision which the
public official will be making, participating in making, using or attempting to use
his/her official position to influence.
Real property is directly involved in a decision if it is the subject of a
goveflrmental decision or if any part of the public official's real property is located within
500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the real property which is the
subject of the govemmental decision. @egulation 18704.2(a).) Since your residential
property is approximately 2,000 feet from the Geyer Quarry, your real property interest is
indirectly involved in the decision. @egulation 18705.2(b).)
"A public official or his or her immediate family are deemed to be directly
involved in a governmental decision which has any financial effect on his or her personal
finances or those of his or her immediate family." (Regulation 18704.5.)
Steps (5) and (6) WiU the financial effect of the decision on the official's economic
interest be material and reasonably foreseeable?
After determining a public official's economic interest, it must be decided
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect
on any of those economic interests. First, the applicable standard of materiality must be
found. Then it must be determined if it is reasonably foreseeable that the effect of the
decision will reach the materiality threshold. Regulation 18706 states: "[a] material
financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable ... if it is substantially
likely that one or more of the materiality standards [citation] applicable to that economic
interest will be met as a result of the govemmental decision."
The materiality standard for real property indirectly involved in a governmental
decision is set forth in regulation 18705.2(b), as follows:
"(b) Indirectly involved real property interests.
(1) Real property, other than leaseholds. The financial effect of a
govemmental decision on real property which is indirectly
involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be
material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are
specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its
financial effect, and the nature of the real property in which the
public official has an economic interest, which make it reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have amateial financial effect
on the real property in which the public official has an interest.
Examples of specific circumstances that will be considered
include, but are not limited to, circumstances where the decision
affects:
(A) The development potential or income producing potential
of the real property in which the official has an economic interest;
(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;
(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not
limited to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of
use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the
neighborhood."
Propane Gas Comoanv Relocation
The Geyer Quarry site is a prime candidate for relocation of two propane storage
tanks and distribution facilities. Because your property is approximately 2,000 feet from
the relocation site, the financial effect of the decision on your property is presumed not to
be material. However, this presumption can be rebutted by specific circumstances that

Regulation I 8705.2(b)( I XA)-(C) address circumstances to consider when
evaluating the financial effect of a decision on property interests. These include the

intensity of use, noise level and air emissions. You specifically expressed concems about

need to be examined to determine if the presumption of materiality is rebutted. Whether

must decide.
We have previously advised that evidence considered in evaluating the
presumption regarding materiality under regulation 18705.2 must consist of reasonable
and objective facts, and that any conclusion reached regarding the presumption must be
objectively defensible. (O'Harra Advice Letter, No. A-00-174.)
We cannot make the necessary factual determination regarding the presumption.
You must apply your specific facts to the above factors in the regulation. If you conclude
that there is no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your property interest,
you may participate in the decision. Conversely, if you conclude that there will be a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your property interest, you may not
participate in the decision. In addition, you may not participate in any decisions
regarding a different relocation site until such time as the Geyer Quarry is no longer
being considered because these decisions would be considered interrelated.3
Habitat Conservation Plan
The Geyer Quarry site is also being targeted for acquisition to provide a
mitigation bank site as a habitat conservation plan for local endangered species in the
area,particularly the Mt. Hermon June Beetle, an endangered insect. Since your home is
located in an area that may contain the beetle, your ability to do an expansion on your
3 For purposes of determining conflicts of interest under the Act, govemmental decisions are
analyzed independently to determine if there will be a foreseeable and material financial effect on a public
official's economic interest. (In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.) Therefore, under certain circumstances,
a public official disqualified from one decision may participate in other related decisions, provided that the
official's participation in the latter does not affect the decision in which he or she has a disqualifying
conflict of interest. ($ueeney Advice Letter, No. A-89-639.) If a governmental decision may be logically
segregated from other related decisions, the public body may procedurally segregate the decision prior to
allowing the public official with a related disqualifying conflict of interest to participate in the decision-
making ptocess. However, where decisions are too interrelated to be considered separately, the official's
conflict in one decision will be disqualifying for the other. (Kilian Advice Letter, No. 4-89-522; Miller
Advice Letter, No. A-82-119.)
home may be affected. You stated that the Geyer Quarry acquisition as a mitigation bank
for the HCP could have an impact on the property value of your home as well as the cost
and timing for obtaining permits to do future expansion work. You also stated that
without acquisition of a mitigation banking site, construction activities will be delayed
and may force individual property owners to attempt to obtain their own individual HCP.
Such plans can take years to process and are very costly. You stated that personal costs
could exceed $250 in ayear.
Regulation 18705.5(a) imposes a materiality standard of $250 in a twelve-month
period on the official's personal finances. It states as follows:
"a) A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal
finances is material if it is at least $250 in arry l2-month period. When
determining whether a govemmental decision has amateial financial effect on a
public official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a
financial effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the
official, nor a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets
and liabilities of a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect
investment interest shall be considered."
Since you stated that the decision regarding the acquisition of the quarry as a
mitigation bank for the HCP could have a very costly effect, certainly in excess of $250,
on your ability to obtain permits for the expansion work on your home, it is reasonably
foreseeable that there will be amateial financial effect on yourpersonal finances.
Therefore, we conclude that the decision to acquire the Geyer Quarry for a
mitigation bank will have a reasonably foreseeable mateial financial effect on your
personal finances, and you may not participate in any of these decisions.
Step (7) "Public Generally" Exception
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate under the "public generally" exception. For this exception to apply, the
decision must affect each of the official's economic interests in substantially the same
manner as it would affect a significant segment of the public. (Regulation 18707.)
Regulation 18707.l sets forth the general rule for the'public generally"
exception, as follows:
"(a) Except as provided in Government Code sections 87102.6 and
87103.5, the material financial effect of a governmental decision on a
public official's economic interests is indistinguishable from its effect on
the public generally ifboth subdivisions (b)(1) and (b)(2) of this
regulation apply.
(b) Significant Segments and Indistinguishable Effects.
(1) Significant Segment. The govemmental decision will affect a
'significant segment' of the public generally if any of the following are
affected as set forth below:
(A) Individuals. For decisions that affect the personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities of a public official or a member of his or her
immediate family, or that affect an individual who is a source of income or
a source of gifts to a public official, the decision also affects:
(i) Ten percent or more of,the population in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency or the district the official represents; or
(ii) 5,000 individuals who are residents of the jurisdiction.
(B) Real Property. For decisions that affect a public official,s real
property interest, the decision also affects:
(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the official represents; or
(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jwisdiction of the
official's a5ency.
fl...fl
(2) substantially the same Manner: The govemmental decision will
affect a public official's economic interest in substantially the same
manner as it will affectthe significant segment identified in subdivision
(bxl) of this regulation."
Propane Gas Companv Relocation
Youprovided facts regarding the "public generally" exception that
apply to the HCP decision. You stated that the same facts do not apply to the
propane tanks relocation. Therefore, the "public generally" exception does not
awly to the decision to relocate the propane tanks to the Geyer euarry site.
Habitat Conservation Plan
You stated that ten percent or more of both the population and the homeowners or
property owners in the jurisdiction are affected by the June Beetle habitat and the HCp
requirements. Therefore, you concluded that asignificant segment is affected and the
HCP decisions will affect your economic interest in substantially the same manner as the
identified significant segment of the public. Howeveq you have not provided facts
supporting your conclusion. Your govemmental decisions pertain to acquiring the Geyer
Quarry as a mitigation bank for the HCP. Without acquisition, construction activities
will be delayed and certain individual property owners may have to obtain individual
HCPs, which may be very costly. You have not indicated how many property owners
may have to seek individual HCPs, but it does not appear that this number includes l0%
or more of the population, homeowners or property owners. Since subdivision (b)(2) is
not satisfied, the 'public generally" exception cannot be applied to the decision regarding
the Geyer Quarry acquisition as a mitigation bank for the HCp.
Step (8) Legally Required Participation
Step eight is an exception that applies when the official is legally required to
participate in the decision. You have not provided any facts to suggest that this exception
applies.

QUESTION: 1. May you participate in decisions regarding the relocation of two propane storage and
distribution facilities since you own residential property approximately 2,000 feet
from the Geyer Quarry relocation site?
2. May you participate in decisions regarding the Geyer Quarry acquisition as a
mitigation bank for the Habitat Conservation Plan since you own residential property
approximately 2,000 feet from the Geyer Quarry?
I Government Code sections 8 1000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Please note that the Commission does not provide advice relating to past conduct. (Regulation
18329(bX8XA).) Therefore, any conclusions contained herein apply only to prospective actions.
r€,

CONCLUSION: 1. Since it is presumpdthat the decisions to relocate the propane gas tanks and
distribution facility to the Geyer Quarry will not have a material financial effect on
your residential property, you may participate in the decisions. Whether this
presumption is rebutted is a factual determination that a public offrcial must make.
You should examine the specific factors in regulation 18705.2(b) that would rebut the
presumption of materiality based on your facts. If you find that there will be a
material financial effect on your property, you may not participate in the relocation
decisions.
2. Since the "public generally" exception does not apply to the decision to acquire the
Geyer Quarry as a mitigation bank for a habitat conservation plan, you may not
participate in decisions pertaining to the Habitat Conservation Plan.

FACTS You are the community development director/deputy city manager for the City of Scotts Valley. Scotts Valley's current population is 11,600 persons. You recently purchased a home, which is located just outside of the corporate limits of Scotts Valley, but within the jurisdiction of the County of Santa Cruz. Your escrow closed on August 2, 2002. The City of Scotts Valley has been working with the County of Santa Cruzto acquire a privately owned property known as the Geyer Quarry. This site is located approximately 2,000 feet from your home. The acquisition of the site by the city and county will address several issues, two of which may create a potential conflict of interest for you.
